content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size Photo by Jackson Sophat on Unsplash 4 min read Just now -- You don’t always need a JavaScript framework. Here’s why HTMX is changing how developers think about the web. About Me Hey! I’m Aditya Ranjan, a 4- ECE student at ADGIPS Delhi (affiliated to GGSIPU) with a CGPA of 8.2+. I’m a MERN Stack + Gen AI developer learning in public. I write about web development and AI, student-to-student, no fluff. Introduction Every developer learning web development gets the same advice. Learn React. Learn hooks. Learn state management. Learn the virtual DOM. Weeks of learning just to build a button that fetches some data. But here’s what nobody tells you, React is overkill for most web applications. A blog, an admin dashboard, a booking system, an internal tool these don’t need a complex JavaScript framework. HTMX is a tiny library that challenges this. It lets you build dynamic, interactive web apps with almost zero JavaScript by letting the server send HTML directly instead of JSON. In 2023, HTMX went viral in the developer community. GitHub stars exploded. Developers who had been using React for years started switching back to simpler server-rendered apps. In this blog, you’ll understand exactly what HTMX is, how it works, how it compares to React, and when to use it. Table of Contents • The Problem with Modern Web Development • What is HTMX? • How HTMX Works • Core HTMX Attributes • HTMX vs React (Honest Comparison) • A Simple Example • When to Use HTMX and When Not To 1. The Problem with Modern Web Development Building a simple feature in React requires a lot of setup useState, useEffect, fetch calls, JSON parsing, error handling, loading states. Most of this complexity exists not because the feature is complex but because of how React is architectured. The browser does all the rendering. The server just sends data. For simple web apps, this is unnecessary overhead. 2. What is HTMX? HTMX is a 14KB JavaScript library that gives HTML new superpowers. Normally HTML can only make server requests through links () and forms (
). HTMX extends this ,any HTML element can make a request to the server and update part of the page with the response. The key difference: the server sends back HTML, not JSON. No JSON parsing. No state management. No component lifecycle. Just HTML going back and forth. 3. How HTMX Works React approach: User clicks button → JavaScript fetches JSON from server → JavaScript converts JSON to HTML → Page updatesHTMX approach: User clicks button → Server sends HTML directly → Page updatesThe server does the rendering. The browser just displays what it receives. This is called hypermedia-driven development an idea from the early web that React and SPAs moved away from. HTMX brings it back. 4. Core HTMX Attributes HTMX works through HTML attributes you don’t write JavaScript, you just add attributes. hx-get / hx-post →make an HTTP request when the element is interacted with hx-target →where should the response HTML go (uses CSS selectors) hx-swap →how should the response replace the target (innerHTML, outerHTML, beforeend) hx-trigger →what event triggers the request (click, keyup, change, every 2s) That’s mostly it. Four attributes cover 90% of what you’ll ever need. 5. HTMX vs React (Honest Comparison) This is not React vs HTMX in all situations. React wins for complex, highly interactive apps. HTMX wins for simpler server-rendered apps where you want to move fast. 6. A Simple Example Live search — type in a box, results appear without page reload.
Your server at /search returns HTML a list of results. HTMX puts it in #results. Done. No useState, No useEffect, No fetch, No JSON,parse, Just HTML attributes. 7. When to Use HTMX and When Not To Use HTMX when: • Building CRUD apps →forms, tables, dashboards • Using a server-side framework →Django, Flask, Laravel, Express • Your team is primarily backend developers • SEO matters • You want fast development without frontend complexity Don’t use HTMX when: • Building highly complex interactive UIs — drag and drop, canvas, complex animations • You need a mobile app alongside web (React Native requires React) • Your team is already deeply invested in React Honest take: Most web apps don’t need React. An admin panel, a booking system, a content site. HTMX plus a good backend framework is faster to build and simpler to maintain. Try HTMX first on your next simple project. You might be surprised. Key Takeaways • HTMX extends HTML with attributes that make HTTP requests and update the page • Server returns →HTML → not JSON →no frontend rendering logic needed • 14KB library, almost zero JavaScript, no build step • Best for CRUD apps, dashboards, server-rendered applications • Not a React replacement a simpler alternative for apps that don’t need React’s complexity • One script tag and you’re building, no setup required NOTE: HTMX works with any backend like Node.js, Python, PHP, Go, anything. The backend just needs to return HTML strings instead of JSON. Official docs at htmx.org are excellent and its best place to learn more. If this made you rethink your frontend stack drop a clap 👏 Drop your questions in the comments I read every single one.
